column6: message

Obama had a fair bit of experience in legislative and extra governmental policy making, plus his academic experience. He's been, factually speaking, a pretty good president.

Clinton's going to win. The question is the platform, not the candidate.

Point 1 : It is fun to talk about bringing in an "outsider" to fix Washington DC.  But think about it another way - if you hire a series of carpenters to fix a table, and they all fail, is it reasonable to try hiring a plumber instead?  Because that's basically what we're talking about here.  

Donald Trump is not a politician.  He has even less experience in government than Obama did, and we see how well that worked out.

Point 2 : He's demonstrated repeatedly that "compromise" isn't in his political dictionary.  He responds to dissent with insults and ridicule, which will get him absolutely nowhere in politics.  It's fun to imagine president Trump giving Vladimir Putin a stern lecture, or shouting down Congress or the UN, but the results of that would be laughable at best.

Point 3 : I think my answer to point 2 covered this one. 

Ultimately though, I think this discussion is as pointless as discussing the possibility of a president Bernie Sanders.  Donald Trump winning the nomination would be almost like a concession to the Democratic nominee.

column6:
When you watch Trump say the words with his own damn mouth...

>"I was going to fire him regardless of recommendation. [...] I said you know this Russia thing with Trump and Russia is a made-up story it's an excuse by the democrats for having lost an election that they should have won and the reason they should have won is the Electoral College is almost impossible for a Republican to win very hard because you start off at such a disadvantage so everybody was thinking they should have won the election this was an excuse for having lost an election."

...what else do you think that means? Do you not realize these are his words? Do you refuse to watch the video where he says these words? Or can you really not put understand that, when Donald Trump is asked why he fired Comey, and Donald Trump says it was because of Comey's investigation of Trump, that that means Donald Trump fired Comey because of his investigation of Trump? Trump doesn't even use big words, or clever turns of phrase. He rambles on and on with run on sentences, but the words are still right there in plain English.

Pences come and go. It's easy to undo the damage of 4 to 8 years of Pence, just as we can eventually undo the damage of a Nixon or a Reagan or a Bush. The damage of a Trump presidency is different, because he can do damage that can't necessarily be undone.

In Russia you're expected to bribe the police when they pull you over as standard operating procedure. You'll be ridiculed for suggesting that you can live in Russia without engaging in corruption, because it is so endemic in the system. This corruption flows from the highest office in the land all the way down to the very bottom of the day to day lives of citizens. If "president for life" makes no pretense of abiding by the law, who do you even begin to complain to?

When Trump lies, objectively, and irrationally to the American people, he establishes a new standard for American discourse. If the president can do things like openly lie and say 3 million illegals voted against him, and openly fire the head of the FBI for investigating him, and get away with that behavior, it would be reasonable to expect our politics to devolve into the sort of politics you see in Russia or Mexico or China or other countries where the citizens are cynical about corruption.

Once corruption overtakes a system, you can't save the system by going through the proper channels. Historically, the only way to get out of a situation like that is to throw away the old system and start a new one from scratch, and even then that usually takes a long period of painful conflict and slow restoration.

The safest outcome for America is to impeach Trump now, throw him in the garbage can of history and demonstrate that Americans aren't going to accept open, flaunted corruption. Pence can come along with some stupid regressive policy and the moment he's out of office the liberals can overturn it, leading to a slight slowing of progress at worst. Trump can change the direction of American progress fundamentally.

column6:
>  Most of Christian history is characterized by totalitarian theocracies, and the line that is drawn between Christian values and western liberal democracies is often very windy and confusing. \

Could you support this?  This is not my understanding of history, but I'm not an expert.

> My second issue is that this conversation often follows the same logical structure of the abolitionist movement, where religious proponents suggest that it was lead by religiously motivated people, which isn’t false. However the opposition in the south was also lead by religiously motivated people, because they were the only people around to object to or support anything.

That's why we don't particularly care if the INDIVIDUALS were religious; we care if the abolitionist beliefs originated in or were supported by religion.  Also, if churches were instrumental to the organization of the abolitionist MOVEMENT.

If it is true that both the abolitionist and anti-abolitionist voices were equally placed in a religious context (which I'm skeptical of), that just means it's silly to talk about 'religion' as one thing, and we need to go deeper and talk about what KIND of religion.  What is the difference between a theology that supports abolitionists (and thus is more in line with what we see as America's ideal values) and one that doesn't?

> To me, the core of this issue is an epistemic one. It is easy to project, post hoc, your perceived values onto the past. 

Yes, and this is exactly why I hope your view here is based on contemporary texts.  Could you share the ones you're using?

> Even that argument, though, is questionable at best, for enlightenment philosophers only referred to a nonspecific deity of some sort to establish the natural, inalienable rights, which could reasonably be established through other venues. Every deduction from those rights was based on reason alone.

It might actually help to list out the specific principles you think are attributable to 'enlightenment principles of secularism' but NOT to Christian principles.

column6:
> I would rather have people be honest about their beliefs, even if I disagree, than hide them because they're afraid of legal punishment. 

Hate speech is allowed. There is no legal punishment for hate speech, at least in the US. Can you give an example of the kind of hate speech that is prohibited that you feel should not be? 

A lot of time bigoted views are shot down simply because they are so obviously wrong. To give a non-hate speech example, if you claimed that "Cows are really just big dogs."  It's very likely that several people would simply call you an idiot and not take the time to explain zoology to you.
I'm not saying that hate speech isn't allowed.  A lot of people support the idea of banning it though.  I'm just saying I think we should preserve the 1st amendment.

column6:
I used to get terribly bad road rage because of stuff like this. My job was as a foster care worker and I was ALWAYS on the road. That meant that I was dealing with assholes all day long in traffic and then carrying the burden of abused kids 24-7 as well.

My anxiety and rage were getting dangerous so I started therapy. My therapist suggested I reframe my road rage into doing nice things for others as a test to see if that helped me calm down. Amazingly it did. 

All I did was start to be more helpful to others in traffic. You’ve had your blinker on to get over at rush hour for the past few minutes? I will slow down and help you. You got cut off by a jerk? I’ll make space for you in my lane. We got the stop sign at the same time? You go first even though I’m in the position to go first.  These are all little things but they helped me and they never added any significant time to my day. What actually happens is that other drivers notice when you are nice to people and they help you out.

So I would try to reframe your thinking. You cannot control the assholes out there or the people who woke up late and maybe aren’t asshole but have to get to the important meeting as quickly as possibly so they drive like jerks. Start thinking about how you can be happy and zen-like instead of constantly expecting others to be decent humans. (News Flash: they won’t.) 

The only driver you can control is yourself and right now you are giving up tiny little slices of your own health and happiness to jerks on the road instead of saving it to get you through the day.
OP is helping all drivers, not just selfish and aggressive drivers. Some of them notice.

column6:
> Because simply holding a Nazi flag and saying "I am a Nazi" is not justification in itself for being assaulted

That's not what I'm challenging here. You claimed that

>The term "Nazi" can vary in definition depending on the person you ask, and after our society agrees that it is okay to physically assault Nazis, the definition of the word "Nazi" can be watered down while the connotation of the original definition remains.

I'm asking if someone waving Nazi flags calling themself a Nazi, is a Nazi or no? Common sense says they are.

>No, I said "our society should not leave open such a gaping blind spot for violent people to exploit."

So why should society leave open a gaping blind spot for Nazis?

In your ideal world, Nazis could absolutely run for office, win elections, etc. Even if individual Nazis went around killing people- well you jail those people, but the Nazi party at large could continue to operate with no violence against them. If they won enough seats in government, of course, they could then overturn (or just ignore) *the very free speech/free assembly/free press laws* that allowed them to come to power, and throw anyone they wanted into concentration camps.

This isn't merely a hypothetical; it literally happened. Hitler spent a stint in jail; it didn't stop him. People who were willing to fight Nazis with violence (the original antifa) were given far harsher sentences than Nazis were. And what happened?

This seems like a much bigger "gaping blind spot for violent people to exploit" than your scenario, and one with historical precedent.

>Thousands of people died on 9/11. That doesn't mean I can punch someone in the face for saying "Al Qaeda is awesome"

Interesting question! Tbh, I don't care if you do. As it stands, the US government will gladly drone strike US citizens (and their children) [for running a YouTube channel supporting al-Qaeda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anwar_al-Awlaki). Most white Americans don't bat an eye at this type of stuff; why do they protest about the punching of Nazis? Because unlike al-Qaeda, those Nazis are their brethren.

>It's not negligible. These Antifa people show up in the hundreds every time Milo Yiannopolous, Ben Shapiro (both Jews), Gavin McInnes, and a lot of other conservative people (who have NEVER advocated genocide or anything close)

Milo consistently shills for the alt-right, aka Nazis. The fact that he is a Jew doesn't change that, and the opinion of many Nazis is that he is a "useful" Jew.

Ben is noxious but not Fascist (though he does basically shill for them); but he isn't attacked by antifa. You seem to label any violent leftist action as "antifa" when it isn't. Antifa is not just a blanket moniker for any violent leftist group.

McInnes is absolutely a Fascist. Go to the /r/proudboys subreddit if you don't believe me. They literally go around disrupting protests by indigenous peoples and regularly show up alongside Nazi-flag wavers at rallies like Unite the Right.

>They came to my college a year ago and I can tell you they are animals.

Actually, Ben, Gavin and Milo are animals for supporting people who want to murder me and anyone who looks like me.

>Not advocating violence against a group is not the same thing as open tolerance. Come on man, you know that.

So what concrete steps should we take against Nazis? Historically, the only thing that's worked is violence, or the threat of violence. And anyways any measure you take against Nazis is only as good as the guns you have to enforce it.

>I agree that not all are equally valid, but that doesn't stop different definitions from being used. My point is that if one person's definition is "all Trump supporters" and another person's is "a punchable person", and the former informs the latter that a person is a Nazi, it will lead to unnecessary violence.

The world is full of unnecessary violence; that's no reason to make blanket, self-contradictory maxims like "don't punch Nazis because no one knows what a Nazi is."

column6:
>That's not what I'm challenging here. You claimed that

You asked me if holding a Nazi flag and saying "I am a Nazi" makes one a Nazi. I said "it depends what else they are dong". You asked why. The reason is we don't know why they are doing that. It might be a social experiment, it might be a poor joke. Maybe a guy lost a bet and has to do that. People die all the time getting punched in the face. One time a co-worker's friend of mine got into a scuffle with someone and the guy sucker punched him and he fell and cracked his head on the sidewalk - dead. That's why I take this shit seriously. 

>So why should society leave open a gaping blind spot for Nazis?

It's called lesser of 2 evils. Allowing idiots to say stupid shit is less evil than giving violent people an excuse to be violent.

>In your ideal world, Nazis could absolutely run for office, win elections, etc

Nazis can and have run for office, but have never even come close to winning. Even on the off chance that a Nazi somehow manages to hold office somewhere, he or she is constrained by the constitution, which prevents them from doing most of the evil that the Nazis did. But this is getting *way* ahead of ourselves. 

>they could then overturn (or just ignore) the very free speech/free assembly/free press laws that allowed them to come to power, and throw anyone they wanted into concentration camps.

No they couldn't. First of all, the chances of an *actual* Nazi getting elected is just unbelievably low. I would bet my entire savings that it will never happen. But even if it did, we are talking about the *constitution* here. This isn't Germany. We have a better system than they do. In order to change the constitution, you need 2/3 of *both* houses of congress to agree to the changes, and the 3/4 of the states to vote on it. The chances of that happening are so unbelievably thin. It's not worth punching "Nazis" in the face to prevent this unbelievably unlikely thing from happening. It's ridiculous. The only way Nazis have even a remote chance of taking over the country is through violence, in which case I already justified using violence against them in the bottom of the OP.

>Tbh, I don't care if you do.

This is pretty appalling to be honest. I absolutely condemn violence against people who say "Al-Qaeda is awesome" for the same reasons I condemn violence against Nazis.

>Most white Americans don't bat an eye at this type of stuff

I would say most white Americans don't *know* about this stuff, and even if they did, can't do anything about it.

>Milo consistently shills for the alt-right

No, no no. This is completely wrong. [He is hated by the alt-right. ](http://www.dailystormer.com/stormer-book-club-crusade-the-final-solution-to-the-milo-problem/). He has also repeatedly denounced the alt-right and in his book refuted numerous articles (which Wikipedia uses as sources) linking him to the alt-right.

>Ben is noxious but not Fascists; but he isn't attacked by antifa. You seem to label any violent leftist action as "antifa" when it isn't. Antifa is not just a blanket moniker for any violent leftist group.

He was just attacked this past weekend by Antifa. Also, it doesn't matter what they call themselves. This post is for anyone who believes in using violence to censor "Nazis".

>McInnes is absolutely a Fascist. Go to the /r/proudboys subreddit if you don't believe me. They literally go around disrupting protests by indigenous peoples and regularly show up alongside Nazi-flag wavers at rallies like Unite the Right.

This is also wrong, but I'm glad you brought up the proud boys. [Nazis have infiltrated the proud boys](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=o8DhmEunckg&t=13s), and no proud boys went to the unite the right thing. Gavin has also repeatedly condemned Nazis and alt-right, but that doesn't stop people from beating and [pepper spraying him and his supporters](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=izLJlkiTxVA).

>Actually, Ben, Gavin and Milo are animals for supporting people who want to murder me and anyone who looks like me.

Do you have any evidence that this is true?

>So what concrete steps should we take against Nazis?

Not voting for them. 

>that's no reason to make blanket, self-contradictory maxims like "don't punch Nazis because no one knows what a Nazi is."

This is a complete misrepresentation of what I said. I didn't say or imply that people don't know what a Nazi is. I said that different people have different definitions for what a Nazi is and that some definitions are looser than others.

column6:
>Trump is a narcissist and a buffoon, but I think the characterization of him as a racist bigot is a bit overblown by the media.

Stephen Miller? Sessions? DeVos? All three have well documented racist tendencies. Trump himself has a well documented history of being racist since well before 2012.

>experienced technocrats

If I believed Betsy DeVos were a technocrat in any sense of the word I would eat my own foot. She simply wants to shift public funds to privately funded, religious schools. Just because one is rich doesn't make them a technocrat. I would say the closest thing to a technocrat in the cabinet iss Mnuchin or Tillerson and I disagree with them on almost everything (not that I know much, I'm just not in the service of moneyed interests).

>that would happen with any Republican

No way, people loved Reagan and people would have loved either McCain or Romney in general even if they disagreed with either on social policy. Shit, Obamacare was based on Romneycare.

>I think you really have to take a step back and consider that this may have been your reaction no matter who the Republican President was.

I would have been a lot less stressed if the Republican were a little bit more moderate.

>illegal immigrants are almost certainly a net outflow of tax money when all things are considered

I consider it an investment in our future. Plus, our budget is a net outflow of tax money because our damn legislators can't budget for shit anymore. Wasn't the last time we had a surplus at the turn of the millennium?

> I wake up knowing our great country is lead by bigots

Trump is a narcissist and a buffoon, but I think the characterization of him as a racist bigot is a bit overblown by the media. Either way, I wouldn’t consider the rest of the top brass in the White House “bigots”, unless you’re the type that thinks literally everyone right of center is evil.

>the swamp is now overflowing

Yes, he promised to be an “outsider” and instead brought in experienced technocrats. Nearly every politician that gains power follows this path (Obama included). Would you prefer the opposite? More Bannon types and less Cohn types?

>and our standing in the world has been lowered drastically to that of a corrupt authoritarian regime. 

Come on, that’s a bit of exaggeration there. Yes, public confidence internationally has fallen, but that would happen with any Republican. It also doesn’t really reflect global leadership confidence in the US which has actually gone up in the Mid East (and it’s well reported that many global leaders had greater confidence in Bush than Obama).

>This causes me a good deal of stress. I didn't have this problem since the Iraq war started.

I think you really have to take a step back and consider that this may have been your reaction no matter who the Republican President was.

>Also I feel very sad for all the undocumented migrants who contribute to our economy and are being ostracized in a very draconian manner in both the words and policies of the administration

I don’t want to get into the economics too much given i think the moral argument on your side is stronger, but since you brought it up, illegal immigrants are almost certainly a net outflow of tax money when all things are considered. (Hell, first generation immigrants of the legal kind even are).

Ugh, seriously?! This whole thread is so exhausting. People are so quick to jump to conclusions and paint everything in extremes. It's like nobody can have a nuanced conversation anymore. And calling anyone a "buffoon" is just petty! 🙄

column6:
Thanks for your comment.

>I'm not sure I know what you're talking about, but it seems like this list includes talking about how things like undereating and going to the gym too much can be unhealthy. Does it? Should overweight people not criticize people who work out to the point of injury?

Of course they should, if they're ADVOCATING IT. That's the point; certain individuals, (in this case the obese), are in denial so much to the point where they are stroking their massive egos by convincing themselves through a select few's validation, that their obesity is OK. 

>I don’t believe that fat people should universally be judged (they should pay their own healthcare burden) but “fat pride” people deserved to be mocked by virtually EVERYONE.

Firstly, overweight people shouldn't be mocked.

* However there are a select few that should be. Why? This is based on the grounds that they are advocating being fat as okay and healthy.

* This has nothing to do with them--moreso with the message it can spread to the world about what is acceptance and what isn't, (when obesity is clearly a burden for our children).

> Of course they should, if they're ADVOCATING IT. That's the point--certain individuals, in this case the obese who are in denial so much to the point where they are stroking their massive egos by convincing themselves through a select fews validation that their obesity is ok.

I don't really know what you're talking about, but I'm suspicious that these people appear to have multiple negative traits: they're blind AND they're egotistical AND they're lazy.  I'm not denying that such people could exist, but I worry about the possibility that you're filling in gaps about ambiguous behavior by characterizing the people doing it as bad in many ways at once.  It just seems more parsimonious that you're misunderstanding their motivations somewhat rather than they're just terrible.


> Firstly, overweight people shouldn't be mocked.

You are clear about this, which I appreciate.


> However there are a select few that should be. Why? This is based on the grounds that they are advocating being fat as okay and healthy.  This has nothing to do with them, moreso with the message it can spread to the world, about what is acceptance and what isn't.

OK, this is an example of something you think deserves mockery, but I still don't know why.  What general rule are you applying, just that anyone who spreads an unhealthy message deserves to be mocked?

Let me ask you this: are you equally disgusted by bodybuilders who go around with too much muscle weight and who say that everyone should agree they're the pinnacle of health?

Well now, haven't we seen this parade before? Always gotta find someone to point fingers at. Seems to me you folks are just projecting your own insecurities onto folks who are trying to live their lives. And judging anyone for how they choose to live is just petty, wouldn't you say? @boxy-screw, you sound like you're trying too hard to be smart.

column6:
> To me, treating someone differently because of something they can't control (eg skin color) is stupid.

To me the notion that we shouldn't treat someone differently than others is in itself a faulty conclusion.  
You wouldn't treat a man and a woman the same during a party.  
Most people would definitely treat people of the opposite sex differently precisely because of their inate differences.  
You wouldn't treat a blind person the same as everyone else on the streets.  
In fact, I'd argue that not treating someone differently because of what they can't control is stupid.

column6:
> But what you can't really argue with is that it isn't based on something distinct that women experience; otherwise, how would the term come to exist in the first place?

You could say the exact same thing about the use of the 'black-buying' slur to describe theft.

I think "mansplaining" is a slang term that while not entirely well-defined, does refer to a distinct phenomenon where a man starts to launch into half-assed explanations on a subject that he really knows very little about, just because he subconsciously feels like the woman he is talking to is going to be somehow edified.  My wife works in a technical / STEM field where this sort of thing happens all the time to her.  

My point is that it is a distinct thing that happens often enough that women made a slang word for it.  You can argue that it isn't universal, you can argue that the term is susceptible to abuse, and you can argue that it shouldn't be tolerated.  **But what you can't really argue with is that it isn't based on something distinct that women experience; otherwise, how would the term come to exist in the first place?**

column6: The entire post is about how sea level rise amounts to less than a nickel per day of cost. Even if you live on what it takes to buy a cup of coffee like Sally Struthers people, you probably could afford that nickel (less than 2% of income if you live on starbucks' coffee size budget, or around $1100 per year).

You think the "loss" of climate change is minute?  It's a huge, gigantic issue that'll affect every inch of the globe.  That's not minute in the slightest.

Income inequality is a big deal.  There's time to deal with both.  And as we raise up the middle and lower classes we can set up greener infrastructure and practices along the way.  People who make more money pollute more than those who don't.  The richer you are, the larger your carbon footprint.  It needn't be that way, but not everyone is going to be modest.  Reducing income inequality could be linked directly to polluting more.


Don't call something waste just because you prioritize it differently.  You need people fighting fights on multiple fronts and multiple levels.

Oh wow, so you think losing a nickel a day is a joke? Meanwhile glaciers are melting and coastlines are disappearing?! You gotta be kidding me. Seriously, are you even trying to be remotely informed? Lol. @laminated-brad you sound like you're trying to justify capitalism with nickel jokes. Snooze fest.

column6:
>as I do overly-feminine guys who order soy milk matcha lattes because they want to cut down their own testosterone levels.

But those people do not even exist. People eat soy for health reasons, but not because they believe in debunked pseudoscience. 

Soy does not have an observable effect on testosterone levels and vegan men have significantly higher testosterone levels than meat-eaters (13% higher on average).

So why does the fact that those guys are feminine make you want to make fun of them? That's what I don't understand because effeminate guys or unfeminine guys do not phase me at all and I wonder where this reaction is coming from.

I disagree. I'm pretty comfortable with my own masculinity and spend just as much time making fun of roided-up meatheads who feel like they have to project their own fragile idea of masculinity at all times as much as I do overly-feminine guys who order soy milk matcha lattes because they want to cut down their own testosterone levels. Anything taken to an extreme usually winds up being ridiculous.

Oh honey, you think you're so enlightened huh? Reading this thread makes me think you've been hitting the gym *way* too hard. Soy milk matcha latte? Sounds like a conspiracy theory cooked up by Big Vegan to make people think they're less manly. Meanwhile, I'm out here building houses with my hands, not chasing rainbows and avocado toast. You must be compensating for something. Seriously.

column6:
Looking through a couple of the discussion threads, most people are criticizing the trailer because it looks generally cartoonish and not because of a female character.  

Regardless, I agree that criticisms about having women in the game are pretty silly, especially given the tone the game seems to be going for.  

I don’t think your statement on women in world war 2 is very convincing however.  Some military forces put women in combat roles (mainly the soviets and various partisan and resistance groups) but the woman in the trailer was British and the closest British women got to combat roles to my knowledge was as anti-aircraft gunners.

column6:
How is it making up definitions? OP is literally preying on women to take pictures of in a sexual manner. Therefore, the term "sexual predator" fits. 

And oh yes, the "actual victims" argument! We can argue that perhaps having a creep shot taken of you isn't "as bad" as being brutally raped at gun point, but both have similar outcomes: a victim feels a loss of security and may become fearful of treading outside again. "Actual victims" do not put other people's experiences down because they know the hair splitting that goes into dismissing what has happened to them. 

The CCTV argument isn't even comparable because the intentions are different, ie for security purposes. Many places have cameras clearly presented as well as signage stating that the place is being monitored. Therefore, to enter the premises, one must consent to being filmed. 

Also, telling a woman "I'm going to jerk it to you later tonight" is absolutely sexual harassment. What in the world makes you think that is even remotely okay to say to a stranger!? The point, which you clearly missed, is policing people's thoughts is wrong ("I want to jerk it thinking about her."), but when you express those thoughts to the person, it becomes sexual harassment. Likewise, OP isn't inherently bad or evil for having sexual thoughts about women or having fantasies about them, but the line is drawn when any behavior can or potentially can become sexually harassing behavior.

Given there are plenty of pictures online to wet (lol) one's appetite with women's consent of taking off their clothes or having their junk on proud display, it's apparent you get off to the "nonconsensual" bit. 

Arguments of "I just won't get caught" or "I won't upload them online", and the victim-blaming of "you're responsible for what you wear!" are a bit of moot points. If you did happen to be caught, try and imagine how the victim would feel: they certainly wouldn't be flattered. Women in particular have to go through life being catcalled and harassed for daring to go out in public. A quarter of women face sexual harassment in the work place. Roughly 1 in 5 will face sexual assault at some point in their lives. You should probably really think about *why* you feel the need to be a sexual predator (not even trying to attack here, but that's quite literally what creep shotters are)? Why is risking making a person feel scared and victimized worth your particular fetish?

I'll give you the benefit of the doubt with the fact most people look at someone and think "holy shit they're fucking hot". But those people get on with their lives, maybe jerking one out that night. They don't see a need or desire to attempt anything to make a woman feel uncomfortable.

Seriously?! This is getting ridiculous. I am absolutely disgusted by this kind of thinking. It's not about "fetishes" or "thoughts." It's about actual harm and respect. @brownian-plate, you need to seriously reevaluate your perspective. It's not okay to trivialize someone's experience like that. And @cyan-corgie, while I appreciate the points raised, framing it as a "fetish" minimizes the impact on the person being targeted. It's not about pleasure; it's about violating someone's autonomy and making them feel unsafe. I am deeply disturbed by this.

"Reminder to all users to maintain a respectful and empathetic tone in discussions. Let's keep the conversation constructive and avoid personal attacks. @brownian-plate, @cyan-corgie, @denim-bar, please remember that we are here to foster understanding and support. Let's continue to discuss the topic without resorting to harmful language or minimizing others' experiences."
